title: Sorting
description: How sorting actually works under the hood: Bubble Sort's simple O(n²) approach, and Merge Sort's divide-and-conquer O(n log n) — explaining why sort() is fast.
objectives: Implement Bubble Sort and understand why it's O(n²)
Understand Merge Sort's divide-and-conquer approach conceptually
Understand why divide-and-conquer gives O(n log n)
Connect this back to why sort() itself is O(n log n)

sections.text: 1. Recap: We Already Use sort() — Now, How Does It Actually Work?
Sorting using sort() covered calling the ready-made STL algorithm. Here we open the hood and see what a sorting algorithm actually does, starting with the simplest possible one.
Bubble Sort repeatedly steps through the array, swapping adjacent elements that are out of order, until no swaps are needed — larger values gradually "bubble" toward the end.

sections.title: How it works
points: The inner loop compares each adjacent pair, swapping if they're out of order
After one full inner pass, the LARGEST remaining element has 'bubbled' to its correct position at the end
The outer loop repeats this, each time needing to check one fewer element at the end (already sorted)
numbers.size() - 1 - i shrinks the range checked each outer pass, since the tail is already settled

sections.text: Recall the nested-loop shape from Time Complexity: an outer loop running roughly n times, each containing an inner loop also running roughly n times, gives O(n²) comparisons in the worst case — exactly why sort() (O(n log n)) is used in practice instead of writing this by hand.
4. A Better Way: Divide and Conquer

sections.title: Merge Sort's core idea
steps: Split the array into two halves
Merge the two sorted halves back into one sorted array

sections.text: Divide and conquer solves a problem by splitting it into smaller subproblems of the same shape, solving those recursively, and combining their results.

Merging two already-sorted halves is a single O(n) pass — walk both halves with two pointers (recall Two Pointers), always taking the smaller of the two current elements. The real cleverness is in the splitting: each split halves the problem size, and merging back up takes O(n) at every level.
5. Why This Gives O(n log n)

rows: log n levels (halving n takes log n steps to reach size 1)
O(n) total merging work per level

sections.text: log n levels of splitting, each doing O(n) total merge work, gives O(n log n) overall — recall this exact complexity class from Big O Notation, and this is precisely why sort() is documented as O(n log n): it's built on this divide-and-conquer idea (or a close relative of it), not a simple nested-loop approach like Bubble Sort.

sections.title: Sorting a deck by repeatedly swapping neighbors vs splitting it among helpers
sections.text: Bubble Sort is like sorting a deck of cards by only ever swapping two adjacent cards, over and over, until nothing's out of order — simple but slow for a large deck. Merge Sort is like splitting the deck among several helpers, having each sort their small pile, and then merging the sorted piles back together two at a time — dramatically faster for a large deck, at the cost of a cleverer process.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing a sorting algorithm by hand for production code instead of using sort() — sort() is heavily optimized and almost always the right practical choice; understanding the algorithm is about knowing WHY, not replacing it.
Confusing Bubble Sort's O(n²) with sort()'s O(n log n) — they are fundamentally different algorithmic approaches.
Missing that merging two sorted halves is itself just an O(n) two-pointer walk, not something mysterious.
Forgetting that log n levels of splitting is what makes divide-and-conquer sorting fast — halving repeatedly reaches size 1 in only log n steps, not n steps.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How does Bubble Sort work, and what is its time complexity?
questions.answer: It repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order elements, letting larger values bubble toward the end over multiple passes; this nested-loop approach is O(n²).

questions.question: What is the divide-and-conquer approach used by Merge Sort?
questions.answer: Split the array into two halves, recursively sort each half, then merge the two sorted halves back together in a single O(n) pass.

questions.question: Why does Merge Sort's divide-and-conquer approach give O(n log n) instead of O(n²)?
questions.answer: Splitting the array in half repeatedly takes log n levels to reach single elements, and each level does O(n) total work merging, giving O(n) × O(log n) = O(n log n).

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Bubble Sort repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order elements across nested loops, giving O(n²) time — simple to understand but slow for large inputs. Merge Sort instead uses divide and conquer: split the array in half, recursively sort each half, then merge the two sorted halves in a single O(n) pass. Because halving the array repeatedly takes only log n levels to reach single elements, and each level does O(n) total merging work, the overall complexity is O(n log n) — exactly the complexity class sort() achieves, which is why it dramatically outperforms a naive O(n²) approach like Bubble Sort on large inputs.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Sorting
sections.text: Bubble Sort = adjacent swaps only, O(n²), simple but slow. Merge Sort = split, sort halves, merge, O(n log n) — log n levels of splitting × O(n) merging per level.

sections.title: Sort an Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums, sort it in ascending order, implementing your own sorting algorithm (not calling a built-in sort).
hints: This is a direct invitation to implement one of this lesson's two algorithms yourself — Bubble Sort or Merge Sort.
Bubble Sort works but its O(n²) time will be too slow for the largest inputs allowed by the constraints.
Merge Sort's divide-and-conquer structure — split, recursively sort each half, merge — achieves the O(n log n) needed to handle the full input size.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Bubble Sort: repeatedly swap adjacent out-of-order elements until no swaps are needed.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Merge Sort: divide and conquer, splitting until single elements, then merging sorted halves back together.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the merge step's temp storage

sections.title: Merge Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: Given two sorted arrays nums1 (with m real elements plus n trailing zeros as extra space) and nums2 (n elements), merge nums2 into nums1 in place so nums1 becomes one sorted array.
hints: This is exactly Merge Sort's merge() step — combining two already-sorted sequences into one.
Merging from the FRONT into nums1 would overwrite elements before they've been read — merging from the BACK avoids that entirely, since the empty trailing space is already there.

Three pointers: one at the end of nums1's real elements (m-1), one at the end of nums2 (n-1), one at the very end of the combined array (m+n-1) — always place the larger of the two candidates at the write pointer, moving inward.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: This lesson's merge() logic, but writing into a separate temp array first, then copying it back into nums1.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(m + n) for the temp array

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Merge from the back, placing the larger of the two remaining candidates at the current write position — no extra array needed.

sections.title: Sort Colors
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums with only values 0, 1, and 2 (representing red, white, blue), sort it in place so same colors are adjacent, in order 0,1,2, in one pass without a library sort.
hints: With only 3 possible values, a general-purpose sort (like Merge Sort) is overkill — counting how many of each value exist is enough.
Simplest approach: count the 0s, 1s, and 2s, then overwrite the array with that many of each in order.
A cleverer one-pass approach (the Dutch National Flag algorithm) uses three pointers — low, mid, high — swapping elements into the correct zone as a single scan proceeds, no counting pass needed.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count the 0s, 1s, and 2s in one pass, then overwrite the array with the correct counts in a second pass.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: The Dutch National Flag algorithm: three pointers partition the array into 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass.

sections.title: Majority Element
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums, return the majority element — the value that appears more than n/2 times. It's guaranteed one always exists.
hints: A value appearing MORE than n/2 times has a guaranteed structural consequence after sorting: it must occupy the exact middle index — think about why fewer than n/2 other elements can possibly crowd it out of that position.
That gives an O(n log n) solution using sort(), but a purely O(n) approach exists too, without sorting at all.

Boyer-Moore voting: keep a 'candidate' and a running count — matching elements increment the count, non-matching ones decrement it; when the count hits 0, switch candidates. The true majority element always survives this process.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each candidate value, count its occurrences across the whole array.

approaches.label: Better
approaches.explanation: Sort the array — the majority element, appearing more than n/2 times, is guaranteed to land at the middle index.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Boyer-Moore voting: a running candidate and count, with mismatches cancelling out — the true majority always survives.

sections.title: Merge Intervals
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the input intervals.
hints: Two intervals can only be checked for overlap easily if they're next to each other in some order — sorting by START value puts every potential overlap adjacent.
After sorting, walk through once: if the current interval's start is <= the last merged interval's end, they overlap — extend the last merged interval's end if needed.
If the current interval's start is beyond the last merged interval's end, it can never merge with anything before it — start a new interval in the result.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Repeatedly scan for any overlapping pair and merge it, restarting the scan, until no overlaps remain.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort by start value first, then merge overlapping intervals in a single forward pass.

sections.title: Largest Number
sections.problemStatement: Given a list of non-negative integers nums, arrange them so as to form the largest possible number, and return it as a string.
hints: This needs a SORT, but not by numeric value — 30 needs to come after 3 ("330" < "3"+"30"... check both orders concatenated).
For any two numbers a and b, compare the STRINGS a+b versus b+a — whichever concatenation is larger tells you which number should come first.
sort() accepts a custom comparator — pass a lambda implementing exactly that 'compare the two possible concatenations' rule.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every permutation of the numbers, concatenate each, and keep the largest result — only feasible for tiny inputs.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: sort() with a custom comparator: for numbers a and b, a should come before b if the concatenation a+b is larger than b+a.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n × k), k = average string length

sections.title: H-Index
sections.problemStatement: Given an array citations where citations[i] is the number of citations for the researcher's i-th paper, return the researcher's h-index — the maximum h such that h papers have each been cited at least h times.

examples.explanation: 3 papers (with 3, 6, 5 citations) have at least 3 citations each.

hints: Sorting citations in DESCENDING order lines up the most-cited papers first — the h-index is about counting how many papers 'keep up' with their own rank.
After sorting descending, at position i (0-indexed), ask: does citations[i] >= i+1? That means at least i+1 papers (this one and everything before it) have at least i+1 citations.
The h-index is the largest such i+1 where that condition still holds — the moment it fails, the answer has already been found.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For each candidate h from n down to 0, count how many papers have at least h citations, returning the first h that qualifies.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort descending, then scan once checking citations[i] >= i+1, stopping at the first failure.
